lines.line: > The speaker, Rachel Miller, begins by examining Yuki's grandparents' passports from Japan. Yuki confirms the passports belonged to her parents, and the conversation reveals that Yuki's parents never spoke English, which led to Yuki learning the language through immersion rather than formal instruction.

metadata.title: Discussing Yuki's Parents' Passports

lines.line: > Yuki recounts her childhood challenges, particularly the difficulty of learning English without siblings to practice with. The speaker learns that Yuki's parents only spoke Japanese, which meant Yuki had to pick up English primarily through listening to others and without direct teaching.

lines.line: > The conversation shifts to the history of Yuki's parents' immigration. It is revealed that Yuki's grandparents came to the United States separately and were married in the U.S. The speaker and Yuki discuss the uncertainty surrounding the details of their meeting and marriage.

metadata.title: Yuki's Parents' Immigration and Marriage
metadata.synopsis: The conversation shifts to the history of Yuki's parents' immigration. It is revealed that Yuki's grandparents came to the United States separately and were married in the U.S. The speaker and Yuki discuss the uncertainty surrounding the details of their meeting and marriage.

lines.line: # Yuki's Schooling and Internment Camp Experience

> Yuki shares her memories of moving to Tule Lake internment camp and her schooling experiences there. The speaker learns about the poor quality of education in the camp and Yuki's subsequent move to Vale, Oregon, where she was able to attend high school.

metadata.title: Yuki's Schooling and Internment Camp Experience

lines.line: > Yuki's parents' journey to becoming naturalized U.S. citizens is discussed, including their excitement about the process. The speaker learns about the family's farming activities near the sugar factory and the types of crops they grew, as well as Yuki's grandmother's entrepreneurial spirit.

metadata.title: Naturalization and Farming Activities

The speaker learns about the family's farming activities near the sugar factory and the types of crops they grew, as well as Yuki's grandmother's entrepreneurial spirit.

lines.line: > Yuki recounts how she met her husband in Vale, Oregon, and the family dynamics following their marriage. The speaker learns about Yuki's husband's background, including his education and internment during the war, and the couple's life working in agriculture and other jobs.

metadata.title: Yuki's Marriage and Family Dynamics
metadata.synopsis: Yuki recounts how she met her husband in Vale, Oregon, and the family dynamics following their marriage. The speaker learns about Yuki's husband's background, including his education and internment during the war, and the couple's life working in agriculture and other jobs.

lines.line: > The discussion turns to Yuki's life after leaving the internment camp, including her education and her parents' work. Yuki describes the process of adjusting to life outside the camp, including the challenges of determining her grade level and the work her family did in agriculture.

metadata.title: Post-Internment Life and Education
metadata.synopsis: The discussion turns to Yuki's life after leaving the internment camp, including her education and her parents' work. Yuki describes the process of adjusting to life outside the camp, including the challenges of determining her grade level and the work her family did in agriculture.

lines.line: # Reverend Cathy's Teaching Career and Education

> Reverend Cathy discusses her career as an elementary school teacher and her education at Northwest Nazarene College. She reflects on her experiences as a Buddhist attending a Nazarene institution and the impact of her first-grade teacher on her decision to pursue teaching.

metadata.title: Reverend Cathy's Teaching Career and Education
metadata.synopsis: Reverend Cathy discusses her career as an elementary school teacher and her education at Northwest Nazarene College. She reflects on her experiences as a Buddhist attending a Nazarene institution and the impact of her first-grade teacher on her decision to pursue teaching.

lines.line: # Reflections on Nampa's Growth and Temple's Future

> The conversation concludes with Yuki and Reverend Cathy reflecting on the significant changes in Nampa, including the loss of farmland and the growth of subdivisions. They express concern about the future of farming in the area and discuss the importance of the Buddhist temple adapting to a changing community and maintaining its history.

children.text: > The speaker, Rachel Miller, begins by examining Yuki's grandparents' passports from Japan. Yuki confirms the passports belonged to her parents, and the conversation reveals that Yuki's parents never spoke English, which led to Yuki learning the language through immersion rather than formal instruction.

> Yuki recounts her childhood challenges, particularly the difficulty of learning English without siblings to practice with. The speaker learns that Yuki's parents only spoke Japanese, which meant Yuki had to pick up English primarily through listening to others and without direct teaching.

> The conversation shifts to the history of Yuki's parents' immigration. It is revealed that Yuki's grandparents came to the United States separately and were married in the U.S. The speaker and Yuki discuss the uncertainty surrounding the details of their meeting and marriage.
# Yuki's Schooling and Internment Camp Experience

> Yuki shares her memories of moving to Tule Lake internment camp and her schooling experiences there. The speaker learns about the poor quality of education in the camp and Yuki's subsequent move to Vale, Oregon, where she was able to attend high school.

> Yuki's parents' journey to becoming naturalized U.S. citizens is discussed, including their excitement about the process. The speaker learns about the family's farming activities near the sugar factory and the types of crops they grew, as well as Yuki's grandmother's entrepreneurial spirit.

> Yuki recounts how she met her husband in Vale, Oregon, and the family dynamics following their marriage. The speaker learns about Yuki's husband's background, including his education and internment during the war, and the couple's life working in agriculture and other jobs.

> The discussion turns to Yuki's life after leaving the internment camp, including her education and her parents' work. Yuki describes the process of adjusting to life outside the camp, including the challenges of determining her grade level and the work her family did in agriculture.
# Reverend Cathy's Teaching Career and Education

> Reverend Cathy discusses her career as an elementary school teacher and her education at Northwest Nazarene College. She reflects on her experiences as a Buddhist attending a Nazarene institution and the impact of her first-grade teacher on her decision to pursue teaching.
# Reflections on Nampa's Growth and Temple's Future

> The conversation concludes with Yuki and Reverend Cathy reflecting on the significant changes in Nampa, including the loss of farmland and the growth of subdivisions. They express concern about the future of farming in the area and discuss the importance of the Buddhist temple adapting to a changing community and maintaining its history.

story.description: The interview, conducted by Rachel Miller, captures the oral history of Reverend Kathy and Yukie, focusing on their experiences as Japanese Americans and their community's transitions in the Treasure Valley. Key topics include their family's immigration from Japan, life during WWII internment at Tule Lake and Minidoka, and the challenges of farming and assimilation in Idaho. It highlights intergenerational changes, the shift from farming to other careers, and the cultural complexity of maintaining Buddhist traditions in a largely Christian area. The narrative also touches on the evolution of Nampa and the impact of urban growth on local agriculture.
